docs.title: SaberCraft Standard
docs.text:
Built for every school

Developed and maintained by Lumina Federation LLC. Originated through the SaberCraft community.

docs.title: SaberCraft Standard
docs.text:
A saber choreography standard for recording, teaching, sharing, and safely performing choreographed saber combat.

Start Here Explore Notation Learn with SaberCraft

docs.title: Start here
docs.text: New to the SaberCraft Standard? Begin with the Start Here guide.

docs.title: More than notation
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard is not just a way to write attacks. It is a public, maintained choreography standard for documenting full saber movement: attacks, parries, body movement, transitions, flourishes, timing, and performance actions.

Learn what makes a standard different

docs.title: For schools and instructors
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard is designed as a shared choreography language.

Schools and performance groups can adopt the notation standard without replacing their own choreography libraries, teaching culture, or curriculum structure.

  • Read the proposal: For Schools and Instructors
  • Learn why notation matters: Why Use Notation Instead of Video Alone?
  • Understand the difference: Standard vs. Notation System

docs.title: What the ecosystem includes
docs.text: Part Purpose SaberCraft Standard The overall public choreography framework SaberCraft Notation The written movement language SaberCraft Core Official baseline Choreographed Movements LUMINA Games Optional game/scoring format Glossary Controlled vocabulary for consistent teaching and documentation

docs.title: Relationship to SaberCraft
docs.text:
SaberCraft is the founding school and original community where the SaberCraft Standard was developed. SaberCraft remains committed to community classes, community events, and training culture.

SaberCraft's CM library represents one implementation of the SaberCraft Standard, but the notation system is designed to support choreography from other schools and communities as well.

Read more at Learn with SaberCraft.

docs.title: Contribute
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard is currently in foundation/rewrite mode.

Future contribution paths may include:

  • corrections
  • notation clarifications
  • CM submissions
  • glossary improvements
  • rules feedback

docs.title: Proposed CM submission format
docs.text: Field Required Proposed CM name/ID Yes Category Yes Saberist A sequence Yes Saberist B sequence Yes Telegraph Optional Lesson/purpose Yes Safety notes Yes Video reference Optional

docs.title: Community growth without losing clarity
docs.text:
SaberCraft is designed to support both:

  • stable official standards
  • community creativity and experimentation

This means schools, instructors, choreographers, and performance groups are encouraged to create original movement libraries using the SaberCraft notation system.

At the same time, not every contribution automatically becomes official baseline material.

This page explains the current contribution philosophy.

docs.title: What can be contributed?
docs.text:
Examples of possible contributions include:

  • original choreography
  • notation examples
  • diagrams
  • teaching notes
  • glossary improvements
  • clarification proposals
  • printable resources
  • beginner teaching material
  • notation edge cases
  • timing conventions
  • archival documentation

docs.title: Schools can remain independent
docs.text:
Using SaberCraft does not require a school to give up:

  • branding
  • curriculum
  • choreography ownership
  • teaching style
  • internal ranking systems
  • performance themes

Schools can publish choreography written in SaberCraft notation while still maintaining their own identity.

docs.title: Contribution categories
docs.text:
Contributions may eventually fall into different categories.

Category Meaning Official Approved baseline standard material Extended Official expansions beyond the beginner baseline Community Independent choreography or contributed material Experimental Draft systems or unreviewed concepts

This structure allows experimentation without destabilizing the core notation standard.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A school might contribute:

  • AA-1 Crosswind Exchange
  • AA-2 Twin Spiral
  • AA-3 Temple Crossing

These sequences could be publicly documented in SaberCraft notation while remaining part of that school’s independent choreography library.

The choreography does not become "owned" by the standard simply because it uses the notation language.

docs.title: Current contribution philosophy
docs.text:
At this stage of development, the project prioritizes:

  • clarity
  • consistency
  • stable terminology
  • readable notation
  • beginner accessibility
  • long-term preservation

Large changes to the notation system itself should be approached carefully.

docs.title: Suggested future submission process
docs.text:
As the standard matures, a future submission process may include:

  1. Public proposal submission
  2. Notation formatting review
  3. Terminology review
  4. Safety review
  5. Community testing
  6. Classification as Official, Extended, Community, or Experimental

The exact process is still evolving.

docs.title: Attribution philosophy
docs.text:
Whenever reasonably possible:

  • contributors should be credited
  • schools should retain attribution
  • historical versions should remain documented
  • choreography origins should remain visible

One long-term goal of SaberCraft is preservation, which includes preserving authorship and historical context.

docs.title: Future possibilities
docs.text:
Future community systems may include:

  • choreography repositories
  • revision history
  • public archives
  • notation libraries
  • printable collections
  • cross-school collaboration
  • federation partnerships
  • community review systems

The current focus is establishing a stable and understandable foundation.

docs.title: Key principle
docs.text: SaberCraft should remain open enough to encourage participation, but structured enough to remain readable and durable over time.

docs.title: Downloads
docs.text: This page will hold official versioned downloads.

docs.title: Planned downloads
docs.text: Download Status SaberCraft Standard v2.0 PDF Planned Quick Start Guide PDF Planned Printable target diagrams Planned CM notation sheets Planned Release archive Planned

docs.title: Release policy
docs.text:
Each official release should include:

  • version number
  • release date
  • changelog
  • PDF manual
  • source link
  • archive link

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: This page answers common questions from students, schools, theater teachers, clubs, and choreographers who are new to SaberCraft.

docs.title: Is the SaberCraft Standard only for SaberCraft?
docs.text:
No.

SaberCraft is the founding school, but the SaberCraft Standard is designed for any group that wants to document choreographed saber movement clearly.

A school can use the SaberCraft Standard without becoming SaberCraft.

docs.title: Do we have to use SaberCraft’s choreography?
docs.text:
No.

SaberCraft has its own Core Movement library, but the SaberCraft Standard is a notation standard, not a franchise.

Your school can create its own forms, drills, scenes, or choreography and write them using SaberCraft Notation.

docs.title: Is SaberCraft only for Star Wars-inspired choreography?
docs.text:
No.

SaberCraft grew from saber choreography, but it also connects to theater, stage combat, martial arts-inspired movement, martial sports, and performance training.

The goal is clear movement documentation, not imitation of one franchise.

docs.title: What problem does SaberCraft solve?
docs.text:
Most choreography is passed down through memory, video, and repeated in-person teaching.

That works until someone forgets a detail, changes the timing, leaves the group, or teaches it differently.

SaberCraft gives groups a way to write choreography down so it can be taught, reviewed, preserved, and shared more consistently.

docs.title: Is SaberCraft just attack notation?
docs.text:
No.

Some notation systems mainly describe where an attack goes.

SaberCraft is designed to describe the larger choreographic exchange.

It can include attacks, parries, defensive movement, turns, spins, ducks, jumps, recoils, locks, holds, thrusts, kicks, flourishes, transitions, and timing between performers.

Attack notation tells you where the saber goes. SaberCraft is built to show what the whole choreography does.

docs.title: What is the difference between notation and a standard?
docs.text:
A notation system helps people write movement down.

A standard gives the community a public reference, shared definitions, version history, and a way to review whether choreography is being written clearly.

Read more: Standard vs. Notation System

docs.title: Is SaberCraft publicly available?
docs.text:
Yes.

The notation material has been available online for over a decade, and this site is being built as the long-term public reference for the SaberCraft Standard.

The goal is for students, teachers, schools, and clubs to have access to the same reference instead of relying on unavailable material or private interpretation.

docs.title: Is SaberCraft free to use?
docs.text:
The public documentation is intended to be readable and usable by schools, clubs, instructors, and choreographers.

For specific reuse, publishing, or redistribution questions, see the Licensing page.

docs.title: Can a theater teacher use SaberCraft for a stage fight?
docs.text:
Yes.

SaberCraft can help theater teachers and student performers document a fight scene so it can be rehearsed safely and repeated consistently.

It does not replace live instruction, fight direction, or safety judgment. It gives the team a written reference for the choreography.

docs.title: Can a high school or college club use SaberCraft?
docs.text:
Yes.

A club can start with the beginner path, learn the six beginner target lines, practice CM-A, and then begin writing short original exchanges.

Start here: Start Here / Beginner Path

docs.title: Can my school create its own choreography library?
docs.text:
Yes.

That is part of the point.

Your school might create its own forms, scenes, drills, or performance sets and write them using SaberCraft.

The shared notation makes choreography easier to read across groups, but each group can keep its own style and identity.

docs.title: How does choreography review work?
docs.text:
Formal review is part of the long-term vision.

A future SaberCraft review council or working group may help evaluate submitted choreography for clarity, safety, notation accuracy, and whether it belongs in the official core library, an extended library, a community archive, or an experimental section.

docs.title: Who maintains SaberCraft?
docs.text:
SaberCraft is currently maintained by Lumina Federation LLC and developed from the SaberCraft teaching community.

The standard is being organized publicly so questions, corrections, and future improvements can be handled in a more transparent way.

docs.title: How do I ask questions?
docs.text:
Use the official website and public community spaces connected to the SaberCraft Standard and SaberCraft.

The goal is for questions to be answerable publicly whenever possible, so other schools and students can benefit from the same clarification.

docs.title: Where should I begin?
docs.text:
If you are new, start here:

  1. Start Here / Beginner Path
  2. Targets
  3. Attacks
  4. Parries
  5. CM-A

If you are a teacher or school leader, also read:

  • For Schools and Instructors
  • Standard vs. Notation System
  • Why Use Notation Instead of Video Alone?

docs.title: Keep your choreography. Share the language.
docs.text:
SaberCraft is a shared notation standard for choreographed saber movement.

It was created so schools, performance groups, clubs, instructors, and independent choreographers can document and exchange choreography using a common movement language — without giving up their own identity, curriculum, or creative style.

You do not need to replace your school.

You do not need to adopt SaberCraft’s entire Core Movement library.

You do not need to teach the same choreography as anyone else.

Instead, SaberCraft gives your group a way to:

  • write choreography clearly
  • preserve movement over time
  • teach more consistently
  • share sequences between instructors
  • archive choreography in a readable format
  • collaborate across schools and communities
  • reduce dependence on memory and video alone

docs.title: Why this is a standard, not just notation
docs.text:
A notation system helps people write movement down.

A standard gives people a public reference they can use, question, review, and build from over time.

That distinction matters for schools. If a movement language is hard to access, unavailable, or dependent on one person’s memory, different groups eventually create their own house rules. SaberCraft is designed to avoid that by making the reference public, maintained, and reviewable.

Read more: Standard vs. Notation System

docs.title: What SaberCraft actually standardizes
docs.text:
SaberCraft standardizes the language of choreography.

This includes:

  • target zones
  • attack notation
  • parry notation
  • body movement
  • defensive movement
  • flourishes
  • timing structure
  • table formatting
  • movement terminology
  • notation grammar

The standard does not require every group to use the same choreography library.

docs.title: Your choreography library remains yours
docs.text:
SaberCraft uses the SaberCraft Standard to teach its own choreography library, including CM-A through CM-X.

Another school might create:

  • Academy Forms
  • Temple Drills
  • Stage Combat Exchanges
  • Tournament Sequences
  • Film Performance Sets
  • Original choreography collections

All of these can be written using the same notation language.

That means schools can exchange readable choreography without becoming copies of one another.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Most choreography communities rely heavily on:

  • memory
  • verbal teaching
  • video references
  • instructor interpretation

That works — until choreography is forgotten, altered, lost, or difficult to reproduce consistently.

SaberCraft exists to make choreography portable.

A written sequence can be:

  • preserved
  • reconstructed
  • translated between instructors
  • reviewed years later
  • taught remotely
  • shared internationally
  • cited in manuals or curriculum

The goal is not to replace live teaching.

The goal is to make live teaching easier to preserve, repeat, and communicate.

docs.title: A shared language, not a franchise
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard is intentionally designed to be broader than a single school.

SaberCraft is the founding school and original development community of the system, but the notation standard is meant to support a wider choreography ecosystem.

Schools using the SaberCraft Standard should still feel like themselves.

Your:

  • ranking systems
  • costumes
  • lore
  • traditions
  • class structure
  • choreography style
  • performance philosophy

can remain entirely your own.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Group Their choreography library Shared notation language SaberCraft CM-A through CM-X SaberCraft Standard Academy A AA-1 through AA-20 SaberCraft Standard Temple Group Temple Drills SaberCraft Standard Stage Team Performance Sets SaberCraft Standard

In this model, the notation language is shared while the choreography libraries remain independent.

docs.title: The long-term vision
docs.text:
SaberCraft is being developed as:

  • a choreography notation standard
  • a preservation framework
  • a teaching reference language
  • a collaborative movement system
  • a public documentation standard for staged saber combat

The long-term goal is to help choreography communities preserve and exchange movement more clearly across schools, generations, and platforms.

docs.title: Learn more
docs.text:
• Understand the difference: Standard vs. Notation System
  • Start with the Notation Overview
  • Review the Targets
  • Explore the Core Movement Library
  • Learn about the founding school at SaberCraft.org

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
This glossary defines the working vocabulary of the SaberCraft Standard.

Term Definition SaberCraft Standard The choreography standard maintained by Lumina Federation LLC and originated through SaberCraft. Saberist The general practitioner term within the SaberCraft Standard — anyone learning or performing SaberCraft Standard choreography. Saberists Plural of Saberist. Lumen A player within LUMINA Games specifically. Reserved for the Games layer — not used for general SaberCraft Standard practitioners (see Saberist). Lumens Plural of Lumen (LUMINA Games players). Duet Two Saberists performing together. CM Choreographed Movement; a reusable choreography block. Telegraph A silent signal used to call or indicate a CM. Attack A choreographed movement toward an intended target line. Parry A defensive response to an attack line. Target A numbered line or point used to describe the intended direction of an attack. SaberCraft Core The official baseline movement library. LUMINA Games Optional structured game/scoring format.

Term decision

The official general practitioner term is Saberist. Lumen is reserved specifically for players within LUMINA Games and should not be used for general SaberCraft Standard practitioners. Avoid legacy alternatives such as Lumeneer or Lumener in official standard text unless discussing historical usage.

docs.title: Learn with SaberCraft
docs.text:
SaberCraft is the founding school and original community where the SaberCraft Standard developed.

For classes, events, community training, and SaberCraft-specific curriculum, visit:

https://sabercraft.org/

docs.title: Relationship
docs.text:
• SaberCraft is the standard.
  • SaberCraft.org is the founding school/community.
  • Lumina Federation LLC maintains the standard.

docs.title: Licensing and Use Policy
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard is intended to be open enough to spread, but controlled enough that the official source, name, marks, and certification remain protected.

Core principle: The knowledge is open. The official name, source, and certification are protected.

This page explains what people may do with public SaberCraft Standard materials, what they must do when reusing them, and what requires written permission from Lumina Federation LLC.

Plain-language summary

You may use, teach, print, and adapt public SaberCraft Standard reference materials with attribution under CC BY-SA 4.0. You may not remove attribution, rebrand the SaberCraft Standard as your own system, or imply official certification, endorsement, or affiliation without permission.

docs.title: Public Materials License
docs.text:
Public-facing SaberCraft Standard reference materials are made available under:

Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International — CC BY-SA 4.0

This applies to public educational materials such as:

  • target diagrams
  • translation sheets
  • notation guides
  • beginner handouts
  • written explanations
  • public instructional resources
  • public curriculum excerpts

Under this license, people may use, share, print, teach from, and adapt these materials, provided they follow the attribution and ShareAlike requirements.

docs.title: What People May Do
docs.text:
Adapting is not rebranding

"Adapt" means adjusting terminology, examples, or formatting to fit local teaching — not printing SaberCraft Standard materials with the attribution and license notice stripped and your school's name put in their place. Adaptations must still credit the SaberCraft Standard and retain the license notice, per What People Must Do below.

People, schools, clubs, chapters, and instructors may:

  • use public SaberCraft Standard materials for education
  • teach from public SaberCraft Standard reference materials
  • print SaberCraft Standard handouts for students, visitors, or workshops
  • adapt the materials for local teaching needs
  • create school-specific mapping sheets that translate their local system into SaberCraft Standard notation
  • say that their materials are adapted from, compatible with, or based on the SaberCraft Standard

docs.title: What People Must Do
docs.text:
Users of public SaberCraft Standard materials must:

  • credit the SaberCraft Standard
  • retain the license notice
  • link or refer back to the official SaberCraft Standard source when practical
  • release adaptations under the same CC BY-SA 4.0 license
  • avoid implying official certification, endorsement, or affiliation unless granted in writing by Lumina Federation LLC

Suggested attribution:

Adapted from the SaberCraft Standard, developed and maintained by Lumina Federation LLC. SaberCraft.org is the founding school/community of the SaberCraft Standard.

docs.title: What Is Not Licensed Under Creative Commons
docs.text:
The Creative Commons license applies to public educational materials. It does not grant free use of brand identity, certification authority, or official endorsement language.

The following remain protected brand assets and are not freely licensed under Creative Commons:

  • SaberCraft Standard
  • SaberCraft Notation
  • SaberCraft
  • LUMINA (LUMINA Games)
  • Lumina Federation
  • official logos

docs.title: Official Certification and Endorsement
docs.text:
Schools may teach from open SaberCraft Standard materials, but they may not claim to be official SaberCraft Standard schools or authorized representatives unless granted written permission by Lumina Federation LLC.

Acceptable language:

Our school uses materials adapted from the SaberCraft Standard.

This worksheet maps our local numbering system to SaberCraft Standard target notation.

Not acceptable without written permission:

We are an official SaberCraft Standard school.

We certify SaberCraft Standard instructors.

This is the official SaberCraft Standard curriculum.

Our version replaces the official SaberCraft Standard.

docs.title: Footer for Public Documents
docs.text:
Use this footer on public SaberCraft Standard PDFs, worksheets, and reference sheets:

© 2026 Lumina Federation LLC. Licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0. SaberCraft Standard, SaberCraft Notation, SaberCraft, and LUMINA are trademarks of Lumina Federation LLC; use does not imply endorsement, certification, or affiliation.

docs.title: Why This Approach
docs.text:
The goal is not to lock the SaberCraft Standard away. A standard only works if people can actually use it.

The goal is controlled openness:

  1. public reference materials remain usable and shareable
  2. attribution keeps the original source visible
  3. adaptations remain open under the same license
  4. official names, marks, badges, and certifications stay protected
  5. the official version remains anchored to Lumina Federation LLC and the public SaberCraft Standard site

The SaberCraft Standard should be easy to adopt honestly and difficult to rebrand dishonestly.

docs.title: Permission Requests
docs.text: For official certification, logo use, commercial publication, formal school affiliation, or other uses not covered by this page, contact Lumina Federation LLC through the official SaberCraft or SaberCraft Standard channels.

docs.title: Standard vs. Notation System
docs.text:
A notation system helps people write movement down.

A standard does more.

A standard gives the community a public rulebook, clear definitions, examples, updates, and a way to check whether choreography is being written correctly.

docs.title: Simple version
docs.text:
A notation system says:

“Here is how to write this move.”

A standard says:

“Here is the public reference everyone can use, learn from, question, and build on.”

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
If a notation system is hard to find, locked behind old material, or depends on one instructor’s memory, groups eventually start making their own rules.

That is understandable.

But over time, it creates confusion.

One school may write a move one way. Another school may write the same move differently. Without a public reference, nobody knows which version is correct.

A standard helps prevent that.

docs.title: What makes a standard different?
docs.text: Feature Why it matters Public People can find it and read it Accessible Students and schools do not need private access to learn it Maintained Someone is responsible for keeping it updated Versioned Changes are tracked over time Reviewable Choreography can be checked for clarity and accuracy Reusable Schools can use it without joining one specific curriculum Durable The system is meant to last

docs.title: More than attack notation
docs.text:
Some notation systems mainly record attack targets. They can tell you where a strike is going, but they may not describe the rest of the scene.

That can create choreography that feels one-directional:

one person attacks, the other person reacts, then the next attack happens.

That can be useful for drills, but staged combat needs more.

A fight scene is not just saber direction. It is body movement, distance, rhythm, safety, reaction, and performance.

SaberCraft is designed to capture the full choreographic exchange.

A SaberCraft sequence can include:

  • attacks
  • parries
  • defensive responses
  • ducks
  • turns
  • spins
  • jumps
  • recoils
  • locks
  • holds
  • thrusts
  • kicks
  • flourishes
  • transitions
  • timing between performers

Attack notation tells you where the saber goes. SaberCraft is built to show what the whole choreography does.

docs.title: Why SaberCraft is different
docs.text:
SaberCraft is built from more than ten years of real practice.

The notation material has been available online for over a decade, and the current SaberCraft Standard is being organized so it can remain publicly available as a long-term reference.

It was developed through SaberCraft, a school that has taught and refined saber choreography for more than a decade with a consistent student base.

That matters.

SaberCraft did not come from theory alone. It came from real classes, real students, real choreography, and years of figuring out what people can actually learn, teach, remember, and perform safely.

docs.title: Built for more than one fandom
docs.text:
SaberCraft may be inspired by saber culture, but it is not limited to one franchise.

It connects to:

  • theater
  • stage combat
  • martial arts-inspired movement
  • martial sports
  • partner choreography
  • performance safety
  • movement documentation
  • teaching systems

At its core, SaberCraft is for people who care about process.

It is for instructors, performers, students, clubs, and schools that want choreography to be clear, repeatable, and teachable.

docs.title: Availability is part of the standard
docs.text:
A standard should not disappear.

It should not depend on unavailable videos, private notes, or one person’s memory.

SaberCraft is designed to be available online, with documentation, examples, FAQs, updates, and public ways to ask questions.

That means students, teachers, and schools can use the same reference instead of guessing.

docs.title: Review and governance
docs.text:
A standard also needs a way to review choreography.

A future SaberCraft review council can help evaluate submitted choreography by asking:

  • Is the notation readable?
  • Are the roles clear?
  • Are the attacks and parries correct?
  • Is the sequence safe to teach?
  • Does it follow the standard?
  • Should it be official, extended, community, or experimental material?

This helps SaberCraft stay consistent as more people use it.

docs.title: Key idea
docs.text:
A notation system helps you write choreography.

A standard helps a community agree on what that writing means.

SaberCraft includes notation, but the goal is bigger:

a public, maintained, reviewable standard for choreographed saber combat.

docs.title: The SaberCraft Standard
docs.text: The SaberCraft Standard defines the public framework for documenting and teaching choreographed saber combat.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
The standard includes:

  • notation grammar
  • target and parry references
  • Choreographed Movement structure
  • core movement library
  • telegraph conventions
  • optional game/rule structure
  • controlled glossary
  • licensing and attribution guidance

docs.title: Normative vs instructional material
docs.text:
Some pages define the standard. Other pages teach the standard.

Type Meaning Normative Defines official SaberCraft terminology, notation, and structure Instructional Helps users learn or teach the system Historical Preserves legacy SaberCraft/SaberCraft Standard material Community User-submitted or non-core material

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text:
The rewritten standard should be released as SaberCraft Standard v2.0.

The original manual and historical pages should be preserved as legacy source material.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
Welcome to the SaberCraft Standard.

This page is the shortest safe path into the system. You do not need to understand the entire standard before you begin. You only need a partner, a safe training space, and enough notation to practice the first core movement: CM-A.

By the end of this page, two beginners should understand how to read a basic SaberCraft Standard table and perform a simple six-step exchange.

What the SaberCraft Standard is

The SaberCraft Standard is a lightsaber choreography standard. It is used to record, teach, share, and safely perform choreographed saber combat.

What the SaberCraft Standard is not

The SaberCraft Standard is not full-contact sparring, dueling, fencing, or combat training for real weapons. It is controlled partner choreography.

docs.title: 1. Safety baseline
docs.text:
Before anything else, agree on safety.

A SaberCraft Standard exchange should be practiced with control. The goal is not to hit your partner. The goal is to create a clean, readable, repeatable movement pattern together.

Minimum practice rules:

  • Use appropriate training sabers or safe props.
  • Check your distance with your partner before starting any sequence.
  • Make eye contact with your partner as the signal that you're both ready to begin.
  • Move slowly when learning.
  • Stop before contact.
  • Do not strike the body.
  • Do not surprise your partner.
  • Reset when either partner feels unsafe or confused.
  • Increase speed only after both partners understand the pattern.

If the movement is not safe, it is not correct.

docs.title: 2. The basic roles
docs.text:
A person who practices the SaberCraft Standard is called a Saberist.

Most beginner examples use two partners:

Role Meaning Saberist A The initiating partner in the written example Saberist B The responding partner in the written example

These roles are labels for reading the notation. They are not ranks, teams, or permanent identities.

docs.title: 3. How a SaberCraft Standard table works
docs.text:
A SaberCraft Standard sequence is usually written as a two-row table.

Each column is one moment in the choreography.

Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Saberist A 1 11 3 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P

The top row shows what Saberist A does. The bottom row shows what Saberist B does.

In this example:

  • 1 means an attack toward target 1.
  • 1P means a parry against target 1.
  • The letter P means parry.

So if Saberist A performs 1, Saberist B responds with 1P.

Here is what those same three steps look like as attack and parry lines:

Target 1 Target 11 Target 3 Attack Parry

docs.title: 4. The first thing to learn: attack and parry
docs.text:
For your first practice session, focus on this simple rule:

A number is an attack. A number plus P is the matching parry.

Examples:

Attack Parry 1 1P 11 11P 3 3P 9 9P 5 5P 7 7P

Do not worry about advanced notation yet. Learn the basic call and response first.

docs.title: 5. Your first core movement: CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A is the first core movement in the SaberCraft Standard.

It teaches the basic relationship between target attacks and matching parries.

Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Step 4 Step 5 Step 6 Saberist A 1 11 3 9 5 7 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P 9P 5P 7P

Read it one column at a time:

Step Saberist A Saberist B 1 Attack 1 Parry 1P 2 Attack 11 Parry 11P 3 Attack 3 Parry 3P 4 Attack 9 Parry 9P 5 Attack 5 Parry 5P 6 Attack 7 Parry 7P

docs.title: 6. First practice drill
docs.text: Practice CM-A in three rounds.

docs.title: Round 1: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Move slowly. Pause after every step.

Say the step out loud:

One. One parry. Eleven. Eleven parry. Three. Three parry.

Continue until Step 6.

docs.title: Round 2: Continuous slow motion
docs.text:
Perform the full sequence without stopping, but keep it slow.

If either partner loses the pattern, stop and restart. Do not improvise yet.

docs.title: Round 3: Clean repetition
docs.text:
Repeat CM-A three times in a row.

The goal is not speed. The goal is clarity.

A clean CM-A should feel controlled, readable, and safe.

docs.title: 7. Switch roles
docs.text:
After Saberist A attacks and Saberist B parries, switch roles.

Now Saberist B performs the attack row and Saberist A performs the parry row.

This matters because the SaberCraft Standard is a shared language. Both partners should learn both sides.

docs.title: 8. What to read next
docs.text:
After CM-A, continue in this order:

  1. Notation Overview
  2. Targets
  3. Parries
  4. CM-A reference page

Do not rush into advanced rules yet. Learn the language first, then build complexity.

docs.title: Quick summary
docs.text:
• A practitioner is called a Saberist.
  • The SaberCraft Standard is choreographed, controlled partner movement.
  • A number is an attack.
  • A number plus P is a parry.
  • CM-A is the first core movement.
  • Safety matters more than speed.

docs.title: Why governance matters
docs.text:
SaberCraft is intended to function as a long-term choreography notation standard.

To remain useful across schools, instructors, communities, and future releases, the standard needs:

  • stable terminology
  • documented revisions
  • controlled updates
  • version tracking
  • clear distinction between official and experimental material

This page defines the current governance model for the SaberCraft Standard.

docs.title: Current standard version
docs.text: Version Status Notes v1.0 Historical foundation Original assembled SaberCraft guide and system draft v2.0 In active rewrite Public standards-focused rewrite hosted at standard.sabercraft.org

docs.title: Governance philosophy
docs.text:
SaberCraft is designed to evolve carefully.

The goal is to allow:

  • improvement
  • expansion
  • experimentation
  • community participation

without constantly breaking compatibility between schools and choreography archives.

Whenever possible:

  • notation should remain readable across versions
  • terminology changes should be documented
  • major structural changes should be deliberate

docs.title: Stewardship
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard is currently:

  • developed by the SaberCraft community
  • maintained by Lumina Federation LLC
  • published publicly at standard.sabercraft.org

SaberCraft serves as the founding school and original implementation community of the standard.

docs.title: Official versus community material
docs.text:
Not all choreography or notation material carries the same authority level.

Category Meaning Official Approved baseline SaberCraft standard material Extended Official expansions beyond the beginner baseline Community Independent or contributed material written using SaberCraft Experimental Draft concepts, test notation, or unreviewed systems

This structure allows schools and instructors to innovate without immediately changing the official baseline.

docs.title: Change philosophy
docs.text:
Small improvements are expected over time.

Examples include:

  • clarifying terminology
  • improving diagrams
  • refining beginner explanations
  • documenting notation edge cases
  • expanding examples

Large structural changes should be approached carefully.

Examples include:

  • changing target numbering
  • redefining notation grammar
  • breaking compatibility with older choreography
  • changing table structure conventions

docs.title: Compatibility goal
docs.text:
One long-term goal of SaberCraft is archival durability.

A choreography written years earlier should remain understandable to future instructors whenever reasonably possible.

Because of that, backwards readability is strongly preferred.

docs.title: Version numbering
docs.text:
The standard currently uses a simple major/minor structure.

Example Meaning v2.0 Major public revision v2.1 Minor compatible improvement v2.1.1 Small correction or clarification

docs.title: Public reference model
docs.text:
The official public reference for the standard currently includes:

  • GitHub repository
  • Markdown source files
  • version-controlled history
  • public documentation site
  • downloadable releases in the future

This structure is intended to improve transparency, preservation, and long-term maintainability.

docs.title: Future governance possibilities
docs.text:
As the standard grows, future governance may include:

  • contribution review processes
  • community proposals
  • standards committees
  • notation working groups
  • federation partnerships
  • public revision logs

The current focus is stability, documentation quality, and foundational clarity.

docs.title: Key principle
docs.text: SaberCraft should evolve slowly enough to remain stable, but openly enough to remain useful.

docs.title: Why Use Notation Instead of Video Alone?
Video is powerful. But video alone is fragile.
docs.text:
Most choreography communities rely almost entirely on:

  • memory
  • demonstration
  • verbal explanation
  • recordings
  • instructor interpretation

That works well for short-term teaching.

But over time, choreography becomes difficult to preserve consistently.

Sequences change.

Details are forgotten.

Different instructors remember different versions.

Videos disappear.

Footwork becomes unclear.

Timing drifts.

And eventually, choreography that once existed clearly becomes fragmented or lost.

SaberCraft was created to solve that problem.

docs.title: Choreography deserves a written language
docs.text:
A written notation system allows movement to become:

  • portable
  • archivable
  • teachable
  • searchable
  • repeatable
  • collaborative
  • easier to preserve over time

The goal is not to replace live teaching.

The goal is to support it.

Video shows performance.

Notation shows structure.

Together, they become far more powerful.

docs.title: Video and notation solve different problems
docs.text:
Video is good for… Notation is good for… Emotion and acting Structural clarity Timing feel Exact sequence reference Performance energy Step-by-step readability Watching movement Reconstructing movement Visual style Archival preservation Demonstration Cross-school communication

A video can inspire choreography.

Notation can preserve it.

docs.title: Why choreography gets lost
docs.text: Many choreography groups eventually encounter the same problems:

docs.title: "We used to teach that sequence differently."
docs.text: Without written notation, small changes accumulate over time.

docs.title: "I know the fight when I see it, but I can’t explain it clearly."
docs.text: A notation system creates a common teaching language.

docs.title: "The original instructor is gone."
docs.text: A written sequence survives beyond a single teacher.

docs.title: "The video angle doesn’t show enough detail."
docs.text: Notation can clarify attack lines, responses, timing, and structure.

docs.title: "Nobody remembers Version 1 anymore."
docs.text: Notation creates historical preservation.

docs.title: SaberCraft is designed for portability
docs.text:
SaberCraft allows choreography to move between:

  • schools
  • instructors
  • teams
  • conventions
  • online communities
  • performance groups

A choreography written in SaberCraft notation can be:

  • printed
  • translated into teaching material
  • reviewed during rehearsal
  • shared digitally
  • referenced years later

docs.title: A shared language across schools
docs.text:
One of the goals of SaberCraft is interoperability.

Different schools can keep their own:

  • curriculum
  • traditions
  • choreography libraries
  • aesthetics
  • teaching style

while still using a shared notation language.

This means choreography can become exchangeable without forcing communities to become identical.

docs.title: A practical example
docs.text:
Imagine two schools:

  • SaberCraft
  • Academy A

Each school has its own choreography library.

Without shared notation:

  • choreography transfer is difficult
  • verbal explanation becomes inconsistent
  • videos require interpretation
  • reconstruction takes longer

With SaberCraft:

  • both schools can read the same notation grammar
  • choreography becomes easier to exchange
  • instructors can discuss movement more precisely
  • sequences become easier to archive and preserve

The choreography remains unique.

The language becomes shared.

docs.title: Written choreography changes how communities grow
docs.text:
Once choreography can be documented consistently, communities gain the ability to:

  • build public archives
  • compare variations
  • preserve historical sequences
  • collaborate remotely
  • publish manuals
  • create structured curriculum
  • annotate revisions
  • standardize judging or review systems
  • maintain choreography across generations

This is one of the reasons notation systems transformed:

  • music
  • dance
  • engineering
  • mathematics
  • software development
  • martial systems

SaberCraft applies that same philosophy to choreographed saber movement.

docs.title: The long-term vision
docs.text:
SaberCraft is not intended to replace creativity.

It exists to help creativity survive.

The standard is designed to support a future where choreographed movement can be:

  • documented clearly
  • exchanged openly
  • preserved responsibly
  • taught consistently
  • studied historically
  • expanded collaboratively

Video captures motion.

Notation captures structure.

Together, they allow choreography to outlive memory alone.

docs.title: Core Movements
docs.text:
Core Movements are reusable choreography blocks. Each movement is a short, repeatable sequence that can be taught, practiced, written in notation, combined with other movements, and performed the same way by Saberists anywhere.

Each movement is identified by a CM code, such as CM-A or CM-B. "CM" is short for Choreography Movement — the original name for these building blocks.

docs.title: Where to Start
docs.text:
If you are new to SaberCraft, focus on the first five movements: CM-A through CM-E. These establish the shared vocabulary, timing, targeting, attack lines, parries, and partner discipline that everything else builds on.

Your first movement, CM-A, is documented right here as the official beginner example. It shows how notation becomes a real, teachable paired movement.

The full Core CM library — including CM-A through CM-E and beyond — is available in the CM Series Catalog.

docs.title: Before You Begin CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A sits at the end of the beginner learning path:

Start Here → Notation Overview → Targets → Attacks → Parries → CM-A

Before working through CM-A, make sure you can recognize target zones, attack notation, and parry notation from the earlier pages. CM-A assumes you have that foundation.

docs.title: How the Movement Library Is Organized
docs.text:
The movement library is organized into four levels, so you always know how official a movement is:

Library Meaning Status Core Official baseline movements used for foundational training Official Extended Official additions that build beyond the beginner core Official Community Movements submitted, shared, or adopted by Saberists and partner groups Community-reviewed Experimental Draft movements, prototypes, or material under review Not yet official

docs.title: Core
docs.text: The baseline movements every Saberist learns first. As a new student, this is your home — start with CM-A through CM-E.

docs.title: Extended
docs.text: Official movements that go beyond the beginner foundation, introducing more complex timing, footwork, transitions, reversals, counters, and cinematic phrasing. Explore these once you are comfortable with the Core movements.

docs.title: Community
docs.text: Movements developed and contributed by Saberists, schools, clubs, and partner communities. Community movements are clearly marked and credit their contributors. Over time, some may be promoted into the Extended or Core libraries.

docs.title: Experimental
docs.text: Drafts, prototypes, and choreography still being evaluated. These are visible so the community can watch ideas develop, but they are not yet official SaberCraft Standard material.

docs.title: CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A is the first official core movement in the SaberCraft Standard.

It teaches the most basic relationship in SaberCraft Notation:

attack line → matching parry

CM-A should be the first paired sequence taught to new Saberists.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
CM-A is designed to teach:

  • the six beginner target lines
  • the meaning of P as parry
  • the two-row notation format
  • safe attack/parry timing
  • role awareness between Saberist A and Saberist B
  • clean repetition before speed

CM-A is not a performance sequence by itself. It is a foundation drill.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before practicing CM-A, both Saberists should understand:

  • Targets
  • Attacks
  • Parries

If either Saberist does not understand those basics, review them before practicing.

docs.title: Notation
docs.text: Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Step 4 Step 5 Step 6 Saberist A 1 11 3 9 5 7 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P 9P 5P 7P

docs.title: Step-by-step reading
docs.text:
Step Saberist A Saberist B 1 Attack target 1 Parry 1P 2 Attack target 11 Parry 11P 3 Attack target 3 Parry 3P 4 Attack target 9 Parry 9P 5 Attack target 5 Parry 5P 6 Attack target 7 Parry 7P

Read each column vertically. Saberist A performs the attack listed in the top row. Saberist B performs the matching parry listed in the bottom row.

docs.title: Training method
docs.text: Practice CM-A in three phases.

docs.title: Phase 1: Static walkthrough
docs.text:
Move one step at a time.

After each step, pause and confirm:

  • both Saberists know the target line
  • the parry matches the attack
  • distance is safe
  • the movement can be repeated

Do not rush this phase. This is where the pattern becomes clear.

docs.title: Phase 2: Slow continuous flow
docs.text:
Perform all six steps without stopping.

Keep the movement slow enough that both Saberists can read every line.

If either Saberist loses the sequence, stop and restart from Step 1.

docs.title: Phase 3: Role switch
docs.text:
Switch roles.

Now Saberist B performs the attack row and Saberist A performs the parry row.

Both Saberists should learn both sides of CM-A.

docs.title: Completion standard
docs.text:
CM-A is complete when:

  • all six steps are performed in order
  • each attack follows the written target line
  • each parry matches the written attack
  • both Saberists maintain safe distance and control
  • the sequence can be repeated without verbal correction

A fast CM-A is not automatically a good CM-A.

A good CM-A is clear, controlled, and repeatable.

docs.title: Teaching note
docs.text:
CM-A should be taught as a language drill, not as a fight.

The teacher’s job is to help both Saberists understand the notation, recognize the target lines, and build safe timing together.

Once CM-A is clean, it can become the foundation for longer combinations, telegraphed calls, and more advanced movement sequences.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Start Here
  • Notation Overview
  • Targets
  • Attacks
  • Parries

docs.title: A fictional example of independent choreography written in SaberCraft notation
docs.text:
This page demonstrates how a school or performance group can use the SaberCraft Standard while maintaining its own choreography library, curriculum, and identity.

The choreography below is fictional.

"Academy A" is an example school created to show how independent groups can document original movement using a shared notation language.

docs.title: The important distinction
docs.text:
SaberCraft is the notation language.

The choreography itself belongs to the school using it.

This means schools can:

  • create their own movement libraries
  • keep their own ranking systems
  • teach their own style
  • preserve their own choreography

while still using a shared notation structure.

docs.title: Example sequence: AA-1 "Crosswind Exchange"
docs.text:
Academy A uses its own naming structure:

  • AA-1
  • AA-2
  • AA-3

instead of SaberCraft’s CM naming structure.

The notation system remains compatible because both schools use SaberCraft.

docs.title: Choreography table
docs.text: Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Step 4 Step 5 Step 6 Step 7 Step 8 Saberist A 1 11 3 3P 9 5 7P 1 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P 9 9P 5P 7 1P

docs.title: Reading the exchange
docs.text:
This sequence demonstrates:

  • alternating initiative
  • attack-to-parry relationships
  • mirrored target responses
  • a simple reversal in Step 4

The exchange begins with straightforward attack/parry structure before shifting initiative during the middle of the sequence.

docs.title: Why this example matters
docs.text:
The goal of this example is not the choreography itself.

The goal is to demonstrate interoperability.

A SaberCraft instructor could read this sequence.

An Academy A instructor could read a SaberCraft CM.

Both schools remain independent.

The notation language becomes shared.

docs.title: A future ecosystem
docs.text:
In a larger choreography ecosystem, schools might eventually publish:

School Example library SaberCraft CM-A through CM-X Academy A AA-1 through AA-20 Temple Group Temple Drills Performance Team Stage Exchanges

Each library remains unique.

SaberCraft provides the common structure that makes the choreography readable across communities.

docs.title: Key takeaway
docs.text:
The choreography is not the standard.

The notation language is the standard.

That distinction allows schools to collaborate without becoming identical.

docs.title: LUMINA Games
docs.text:
LUMINA Games are the optional play/scoring format built on top of the notation and CM system.

The standard does not require competitive play. Games are a structured way to practice recall, timing, role switching, and choreographed communication.

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
• Rules
  • Scoring
  • Power Plays

docs.title: Power Plays
docs.text:
Power Plays are advanced game elements used in LUMINA Games.

Migration needed

Pull current Power Play definitions from the existing rules pages and rewrite them into a clear reference format.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
This page will become the single authoritative rules page for LUMINA Games.

Migration needed

Merge and clean content from the existing SaberCraft LUMINA Overview and Advanced Rules pages.

docs.title: Starter rule flow
docs.text:
1. Two Lumens enter the play area.
  2. One Lumen calls or telegraphs a CM.
  3. Both Lumens perform the CM.
  4. Successful completion scores according to the ruleset.
  5. Roles switch.
  6. Play continues until the round ends.

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text: This page will define the official scoring reference for LUMINA Games.

docs.title: Working principle
docs.text:
Scoring should reward safe, recognizable completion of choreographed movement.

Migration needed

Consolidate scoring rules from the existing LUMINA Overview and Advanced Rules pages.

docs.title: SaberCraft Notation
docs.text:
SaberCraft Notation is the written movement language of the SaberCraft Standard.

It helps students, instructors, clubs, schools, and performance groups write choreographed saber movement clearly enough to teach, rehearse, review, and preserve.

Plain-English version

SaberCraft Notation is a choreography map. It tells each Saberist what to do at each step of a sequence.

docs.title: What to learn first
docs.text:
Do not start by memorizing the full notation legend.

Start with the beginner core:

  1. six beginner target lines
  2. basic attacks
  3. matching parries
  4. the two-Saberist table format
  5. CM-A, the first core movement

That is enough to read and practice your first SaberCraft Standard sequence.

The full notation reference is available later in the Notation Legend.

docs.title: The beginner target set
docs.text:
The beginner path uses six target lines:

Target Beginner description 1 High / upper center line 11 High opposite line 3 Side line 9 Opposite side line 5 Low line 7 Opposite low line

These six lines are used in CM-A and give new Saberists a simple starting vocabulary.

For more detail, see Targets.

docs.title: Attacks and parries
docs.text:
The simplest SaberCraft Standard notation uses numbers and the letter P.

Symbol type Meaning Number Attack toward that target line Number + P Parry against that target line

Examples:

Attack Matching parry 1 1P 11 11P 3 3P 9 9P 5 5P 7 7P

This creates the first call-and-response pattern:

attack line → matching parry

For more detail, see Attacks and Parries.

docs.title: The two-Saberist table
docs.text:
Most beginner SaberCraft Standard sequences are written as a two-row table.

One row belongs to each Saberist. Each column is one step in the choreography.

Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Saberist A 1 11 3 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P

Read the table vertically, one column at a time.

  • Step 1: Saberist A attacks 1; Saberist B parries 1P.
  • Step 2: Saberist A attacks 11; Saberist B parries 11P.
  • Step 3: Saberist A attacks 3; Saberist B parries 3P.

The table keeps both performers connected to the same timing.

docs.title: First complete example: CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A is the first official core movement sequence.

Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Step 4 Step 5 Step 6 Saberist A 1 11 3 9 5 7 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P 9P 5P 7P

CM-A teaches the basic attack/parry relationship across the six beginner lines.

For the full teaching page, see CM-A.

docs.title: More than attack notation
docs.text:
The SaberCraft Standard can document more than attack targets.

As choreography becomes more advanced, notation can include defensive movement, body movement, turns, spins, jumps, locks, holds, flourishes, transitions, and other performance actions.

Beginner students do not need all of that at first.

Learn the six-line attack/parry foundation first. Then use the Notation Legend as a reference when choreography needs more detail.

docs.title: What notation does not replace
docs.text:
Notation is powerful, but it does not replace live teaching.

A notation table may not fully show:

  • speed
  • distance
  • acting intention
  • emotional tone
  • exact footwork
  • safety adjustments
  • camera angle or staging

Use notation with instruction, rehearsal, video, or coaching when

docs.title: Attacks
docs.text:
An attack is a choreographed movement toward a numbered target line.

In SaberCraft Notation, a plain target number means that the Saberist is performing an attack toward that line.

Important

An attack in the SaberCraft Standard is not permission to strike your partner. It is a controlled movement in a shared choreography.

docs.title: Basic attack notation
docs.text:
A plain number is an attack.

Symbol Meaning 1 Attack target 1 11 Attack target 11 3 Attack target 3 9 Attack target 9 5 Attack target 5 7 Attack target 7

The number tells the reader which target line the movement is directed toward.

For the full twelve-line attack reference, use the Notation Legend.

docs.title: Attack versus target
docs.text:
The target is the line.

The attack is the action moving toward that line.

Concept Meaning Target The numbered line being used Attack The choreographed movement toward that line

Example:

3 means the Saberist attacks target line 3.

docs.title: Attack and parry relationship
docs.text:
Most beginner attacks have a matching parry.

Attack Matching parry 1 1P 11 11P 3 3P 9 9P 5 5P 7 7P

The attack gives the line. The parry receives or answers that line.

docs.title: Attacks in a two-Saberist table
docs.text:
In a SaberCraft Standard table, the attack appears in the step column for the Saberist performing it.

Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Saberist A 1 11 3 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P

Read this vertically:

  • Step 1: Saberist A attacks 1; Saberist B parries 1P.
  • Step 2: Saberist A attacks 11; Saberist B parries 11P.
  • Step 3: Saberist A attacks 3; Saberist B parries 3P.

docs.title: Control standard
docs.text:
A SaberCraft Standard attack should be:

  • intentional
  • readable
  • repeatable
  • controlled
  • safe for the partner

The attack should move toward the target line and stop before contact unless a specific, safe, pre-agreed theatrical contact rule is being used.

For beginner documentation, assume no contact.

docs.title: Common beginner mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Correction Swinging too hard Slow down and make the line readable Aiming at the body Aim toward the choreographed line, not the person Changing the line mid-swing Follow the written target Moving before the partner is ready Reset and confirm both Saberists understand the step Treating notation like sparring Keep it choreographed and repeatable

docs.title: Attacks in CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A uses six beginner attacks: 1, 11, 3, 9, 5, and 7.

For the full six-step CM-A table, use the CM-A reference page.

Together, these teach a basic tour of the beginner target lines.

docs.title: Teaching note
docs.text:
When teaching attacks, do not start with speed. Start with shape.

A good beginner attack should answer three questions clearly:

  1. What target line is being used?
  2. Can the partner read it?
  3. Can both Saberists repeat it safely?

If the answer is no, the attack is not ready to speed up.

docs.title: Attack diagrams
docs.text: Attack 1 Attack 11 Attack 3 Attack 9 Attack 5 Attack 7

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Targets
  • Parries
  • Notation Examples
  • CM-A

docs.title: CM-A
docs.text: Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Step 4 Step 5 Step 6 Saberist A 1 11 3 9 5 7 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P 9P 5P 7P

docs.title: Reading the table
docs.text:
Each column is one moment in the choreography.

  • Step 1: A attacks 1; B performs 1P
  • Step 2: A attacks 11; B performs 11P
  • Step 3: A attacks 3; B performs 3P

docs.title: Notation Legend
docs.text:
This page is the reference table for SaberCraft notation symbols.

For first-time readers, start with the six beginner lines in Targets, Attacks, and Parries. The tables below include the wider twelve-line reference for notation lookup.

Reference page

This page is meant for lookup. Beginners do not need to memorize every symbol before practicing CM-A.

docs.title: Beginner symbols
docs.text:
These are the first symbols most Saberists should learn.

Symbol Meaning Status 1 Attack target 1 Beginner core 1P Parry target 1 Beginner core 11 Attack target 11 Beginner core 11P Parry target 11 Beginner core 3 Attack target 3 Beginner core 3P Parry target 3 Beginner core 9 Attack target 9 Beginner core 9P Parry target 9 Beginner core 5 Attack target 5 Beginner core 5P Parry target 5 Beginner core 7 Attack target 7 Beginner core 7P Parry target 7 Beginner core

docs.title: Full attack reference
docs.text:
A plain number is an attack toward that target line.

Symbol Meaning Status 1 Attack target 1 Beginner core 2 Attack target 2 Full reference 3 Attack target 3 Beginner core 4 Attack target 4 Full reference 5 Attack target 5 Beginner core 6 Attack target 6 Full reference 7 Attack target 7 Beginner core 8 Attack target 8 Full reference 9 Attack target 9 Beginner core 10 Attack target 10 Full reference 11 Attack target 11 Beginner core 12 Attack target 12 Full reference

docs.title: Full parry reference
docs.text:
A number followed by P is a parry against that target line.

Symbol Meaning Status 1P Parry target 1 Beginner core 2P Parry target 2 Full reference 3P Parry target 3 Beginner core 4P Parry target 4 Full reference 5P Parry target 5 Beginner core 6P Parry target 6 Full reference 7P Parry target 7 Beginner core 8P Parry target 8 Full reference 9P Parry target 9 Beginner core 10P Parry target 10 Full reference 11P Parry target 11 Beginner core 12P Parry target 12 Full reference

docs.title: Expanded movement symbols
docs.text:
These symbols are part of the broader SaberCraft notation vocabulary. They are reference terms, not beginner requirements.

Symbol Meaning Status P Parry / block marker Core notation S CW Spin right / clockwise Expanded reference S CCW Spin left / counterclockwise Expanded reference T180-cw Turn 180 degrees clockwise Expanded reference D Duck Expanded reference Th Forward thrust Expanded reference X Lock Expanded reference S+S Saber stand-off / prolonged lock Expanded reference H Hold Expanded reference Pt Pitch Expanded reference M Modifier Expanded reference J Hop / jump Expanded reference Re Recoil / bounce back Expanded reference K Kick Expanded reference Bl Blend Expanded reference

docs.title: Flourish symbols
docs.text:
Flourish notation describes stylized saber movement layered into choreography.

Symbol Meaning Status Fl-Rev Reverse flourish Expanded reference Fl-Fwd Forward flourish Expanded reference Fl-F8 Forward figure-eight flourish Expanded reference Fl-R8 Reverse figure-eight flourish Expanded reference Fl-Sb Saber flourish Expanded reference Fl-H Hand flourish Expanded reference Fl-P Pommel flourish Expanded reference Fl-RP Reverse pommel flourish Expanded reference Fl-OA Over-arm flourish Expanded reference

docs.title: Terms needing confirmation
docs.text:
The following terms have been mentioned in discussion but are not yet confirmed from the current project source material.

Term Status Note Pop Needs confirmation Add once the official symbol and definition are confirmed Bash Needs confirmation Add once the official symbol and definition are confirmed

docs.title: Parries
docs.text:
A parry is a choreographed defensive response to an attack line.

In SaberCraft Notation, a parry is written as the target number plus the letter P.

Plain-English version

If an attack is written as 1, the matching parry is written as 1P.

docs.title: Basic parry notation
docs.text:
A number followed by P is a parry.

Symbol Meaning 1P Parry target 1 11P Parry target 11 3P Parry target 3 9P Parry target 9 5P Parry target 5 7P Parry target 7

The number tells the reader which target line is being received or answered.

The P tells the reader that the action is a parry, not an attack.

For the full twelve-line parry reference, use the Notation Legend.

docs.title: Parry versus block
docs.text:
In casual teaching, people may say "block."

In official SaberCraft Standard notation, use parry for the written P response.

Term Use Parry Preferred official notation term Block Informal teaching word; avoid as the main standard term

This keeps the notation clear and consistent.

docs.title: Attack and parry relationship
docs.text:
Beginner notation uses matching attack/parry pairs.

Attack Matching parry 1 1P 11 11P 3 3P 9 9P 5 5P 7 7P

The attack creates the line. The parry answers that line.

docs.title: Parries in a two-Saberist table
docs.text:
In a SaberCraft Standard table, the parry appears in the step column for the Saberist performing the defensive response.

Player Step 1 Step 2 Step 3 Saberist A 1 11 3 Saberist B 1P 11P 3P

Read this vertically:

  • Step 1: Saberist A attacks 1; Saberist B parries 1P.
  • Step 2: Saberist A attacks 11; Saberist B parries 11P.
  • Step 3: Saberist A attacks 3; Saberist B parries 3P.

docs.title: Control standard
docs.text:
A SaberCraft Standard parry should be:

  • ready
  • controlled
  • readable
  • repeatable
  • safe for both Saberists
  • matched to the intended attack line

The purpose of the parry is not to win a fight. The purpose is to complete the shared choreographic phrase safely and clearly.

docs.title: Common beginner mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Correction Waiting too long to move Start the parry as part of the shared timing Swatting at the attack Meet the line with control Overpowering the partner Reduce force and preserve choreography Parrying the wrong line Slow down and match the written target Treating the parry like a real fight response Keep it theatrical, repeatable, and safe

docs.title: Parries in CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A uses six matching parries: 1P, 11P, 3P, 9P, 5P, and 7P.

For the full six-step CM-A table, use the CM-A reference page.

Together, these teach the basic defensive response pattern for the six beginner target lines.

docs.title: Teaching note
docs.text:
When teaching parries, focus on recognition before speed.

A good beginner parry should answer three questions clearly:

  1. Which attack line is being received?
  2. Does the parry match the written notation?
  3. Can both Saberists repeat the exchange safely?

If the answer is no, slow down and rebuild the step.

docs.title: Parry diagrams
docs.text: Parry 1 Parry 11 Parry 3 Parry 9 Parry 5 Parry 7

docs.title: Targets
docs.text:
Targets are the numbered lines used in SaberCraft Notation to describe where an attack is directed.

The SaberCraft Standard uses a clock-face model. Imagine a clock placed in front of your partner. The number in the notation points to the intended line of movement.

Beginner idea

A target number does not mean "hit this body part." It means "move toward this choreographed line."

docs.title: The six beginner targets
docs.text:
The beginner target set uses six lines:

Target Beginner description Common use 1 High / upper center line Opens the first vertical line of CM-A 11 High opposite line Trains the opposite high response 3 Side line Trains a horizontal side response 9 Opposite side line Trains the opposite horizontal side response 5 Low line Trains a lower diagonal or low-line response 7 Opposite low line Trains the opposite low-line response

These six targets are enough to read and practice CM-A, the first core movement. The full twelve-line notation reference is available in the Notation Legend.

docs.title: How to read a target
docs.text:
A plain number is an attack toward that target line.

Symbol Meaning 1 Attack target 1 11 Attack target 11 3 Attack target 3

A number followed by P is a parry against that target line.

Symbol Meaning 1P Parry target 1 11P Parry target 11 3P Parry target 3

docs.title: Target perspective
docs.text:
Targets are read from the perspective of the choreographed exchange, not as random screen directions.

When learning, do not overthink camera angle, audience angle, or left/right naming. Start with the shared clock-face idea between the two Saberists.

The important questions are:

  1. Which target line is being attacked?
  2. Which parry receives that line?
  3. Can both Saberists repeat the same movement safely?

docs.title: Targets in CM-A
docs.text:
CM-A uses all six beginner targets in sequence.

For the full six-step table, use the CM-A reference page. This page only introduces the target concept so new readers are not asked to learn the whole sequence in multiple places.

This teaches the basic pattern:

attack line → matching parry

docs.title: Control and distance
docs.text:
A target is not a license to strike.

In beginner practice:

  • the attack should be controlled
  • the defender should be ready
  • the saber should stop before contact
  • both Saberists should understand the line before adding speed

If a target line causes confusion or unsafe distance, slow down and reset.
